title: Microsoft SQL Server Elevation of Privilege Zero-Day Grants Sysadmin Access via TDS Protocol Flaw: March 2026 Patch Tuesday (CVE-2026-21262)

sources.name: Microsoft Security Response Center - CVE-2026-21262 Advisory
Tenable: Microsoft's March 2026 Patch Tuesday Addresses 83 CVEs (CVE-2026-21262, CVE-2026-26127)
Qualys: Microsoft Patch Tuesday March 2026 Security Update Review
Arctic Wolf: Microsoft Patch Tuesday March 2026
BleepingComputer: Microsoft March 2026 Patch Tuesday Fixes 2 Zero-Days, 79 Flaws
The Hacker News: Microsoft Patches 84 Flaws in March Patch Tuesday, Including Two Public Zero-Days
Malwarebytes: March 2026 Patch Tuesday Fixes Two Zero-Day Vulnerabilities
Security Boulevard: Microsoft SQL Server EoP Vulnerability CVE-2026-21262
LufSec: Microsoft SQL Server CVE-2026-21262 Zero-Day Exposes Privilege Escalation Risk

timeline.event: Vulnerability publicly disclosed as a zero-day by security researcher Erland Sommarskog prior to patching
Microsoft March 2026 Patch Tuesday released - patches available for all affected SQL Server versions (2016-2025)
CVE-2026-21262 officially assigned CVSS 8.8 (High); no active exploitation reported
No known public PoC or active exploitation confirmed; EPSS at 0.08%

simple_summary.what_happened: A security researcher named Erland Sommarskog publicly disclosed a flaw in Microsoft SQL Server before a patch was ready, making this a zero-day. The bug exists in how SQL Server handles the login handshake (TDS protocol). Any user with the most basic database account can exploit this to silently promote themselves to a database 'sysadmin' — the highest possible privilege level — over the network. Microsoft patched this on March 10, 2026 as part of its monthly Patch Tuesday release, covering SQL Server versions from 2016 through 2025.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: All organizations running Microsoft SQL Server 2016, 2017, 2019, 2022, or 2025 on any edition (Standard, Enterprise, Express, Developer) on both Windows and Linux. This is one of the most widely deployed database platforms in the enterprise world.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker obtains any valid SQL Server login — even the most basic account used by an application or a developer. Step 2: Attacker connects remotely to the SQL Server on port 1433. Step 3: During the login handshake, attacker sends a specially crafted FeatureExt payload inside the TDS protocol's LOGIN7 record. Step 4: The server's flawed validation logic assigns the attacker's session a sysadmin role instead of the requested low-privilege role. Step 5: Attacker now has unrestricted control over all databases — can read, modify, or delete any data, create new admin accounts, and potentially pivot to other systems connected to the database.
what_you_should_do: Apply the March 2026 security patch immediately — for CU track servers, install the latest cumulative update (KB5077464 for SQL 2022, KB5077469 for SQL 2019, etc.); for GDR track, install the GDR patch
Audit all SQL Server logins and remove unnecessary or stale user accounts that could be used as attack entry points
Restrict TCP 1433 access using firewall rules so only authorized applications and administrators can reach SQL Server instances
Enable SQL Server Audit on all authentication and role change events to detect any exploitation attempts or unexpected privilege elevations
Review all application service accounts to ensure they use the minimum required permissions
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: SQL Server stores the most sensitive data in most organizations — customer records, financial transactions, credentials, intellectual property. A sysadmin compromise means total database loss: an attacker can exfiltrate everything, modify or corrupt records, drop databases entirely, or use the database server as a foothold for broader network compromise. Because this was a zero-day (publicly known before the patch), threat actors had a brief window to develop exploits before organizations could patch.

tactics.description: Attackers leverage valid low-privilege SQL Server credentials to trigger the flaw and escalate to sysadmin-level access
SQL Server's TDS authentication protocol is exposed on the network; the flaw is triggered during the login handshake
With sysadmin access, attackers can access all databases including those storing credentials, secrets, and sensitive records
Sysadmin-level access allows creation of new SQL Server logins for persistent backdoor access

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on identifying unexpected privilege escalation within SQL Server — specifically, sessions where a low-privileged login suddenly gains or exercises sysadmin-level permissions. Enable SQL Server Audit and Extended Events to capture authentication anomalies and role changes.

log_sources.description: Captures login events, permission changes, and DDL operations; required to detect privilege escalation and post-exploitation activity
Contains authentication failures and unusual login patterns that may indicate exploitation attempts
Network logon events (Event ID 4624/4625) for authentication monitoring at the OS level
Monitor TDS protocol traffic on port 1433 for unusual connection patterns or malformed handshake payloads

what_to_look_for.description: A login assigned public or limited roles executing DBA-level T-SQL (e.g., sp_configure, CREATE LOGIN, xp_cmdshell)
what_to_look_for.log_field: SQL Server Audit: action_id='CL' OR 'CO' from unexpected principals

what_to_look_for.indicator: Role membership changes without corresponding GRANT statements
what_to_look_for.description: Accounts suddenly appearing in sysadmin role without a preceding ALTER SERVER ROLE event
what_to_look_for.log_field: SQL Server Audit: class_type='LO' and role_name='sysadmin'

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unusual LOGIN7 / FeatureExt payloads in TDS traffic
what_to_look_for.description: Malformed or oversized FeatureExt payloads during TDS pre-login and login phases, potentially indicating exploitation
what_to_look_for.log_field: Network IDS: TCP port 1433, TDS protocol anomaly

what_to_look_for.description: SQL Server connections from IP addresses not matching known application servers, admin workstations, or jump hosts
what_to_look_for.log_field: SQL Server Error Log: Login succeeded for user from [unexpected IP]

immediate_actions.action: Apply March 2026 SQL Server Security Patches Immediately
immediate_actions.description: Install the appropriate patch for your SQL Server version and servicing track. CU track: KB5077464 (SQL 2022 CU23), KB5077469 (SQL 2019 CU32), KB5077471 (SQL 2017 CU31), KB5077466 (SQL 2025 CU2). GDR track: KB5077465 (SQL 2022), KB5077470 (SQL 2019), KB5077472 (SQL 2017), KB5077474 (SQL 2016), KB5077468 (SQL 2025).
immediate_actions.command: SELECT @@VERSION -- Run after patch to verify updated version

immediate_actions.description: Use host-based or network firewall rules to limit inbound access to TCP port 1433 to only authorized application servers and administrative workstations. This reduces the attack surface even on unpatched systems.
immediate_actions.command: netsh advfirewall firewall add rule name='SQL Restrict' protocol=TCP dir=in localport=1433 action=block

immediate_actions.action: Enable SQL Server Audit for Authentication and Role Events
immediate_actions.description: Enable SQL Server Audit to log all authentication events and server-level role membership changes. This is essential to detect any exploitation attempts and post-compromise activity.
immediate_actions.command: CREATE SERVER AUDIT SQLServerAudit TO FILE (FILEPATH='C:\SQLAudit\'); ALTER SERVER AUDIT SQLServerAudit WITH (STATE=ON);

immediate_actions.action: Audit and Minimize SQL Server Logins
immediate_actions.description: Review all SQL Server logins and remove stale, unused, or overly-permissive accounts. The vulnerability requires valid credentials — reducing the number of valid accounts limits the attack surface.
immediate_actions.command: SELECT name, type_desc, is_disabled FROM sys.server_principals WHERE type IN ('S','U') ORDER BY name;
